title: Mutual aid skills directory
summary: A privacy-respecting list of useful skills, tools, and availability in a neighborhood or small group.
content: A skills directory helps find help without every task depending on one person. List skills such as first aid training, childcare, translation, cooking, repair, driving, animal care, gardening, radio use, and elder support. Record availability and limits. Keep sensitive details private and avoid turning the list into forced labor. For Mutual aid skills directory, the useful record should include the date, the person responsible, what was checked, what stayed uncertain, and the next safe action. Review mutual aid skills directory after weather changes, illness changes, new damage, new supplies, or movement to another place, because small changes can make an old decision unsafe.
materials: Clean storage space. Context: “Mutual aid skills directory”.
steps: Ask for voluntary skills and limits.
Create backup people for critical tasks.
Update when people are tired or unavailable.
warnings: Do not pressure untrained people into dangerous tasks.
Do not publish private contact details publicly.
common_mistakes: Treating Mutual aid skills directory as finished after the first attempt instead of writing the next review time.
Moving people or supplies for Mutual aid skills directory without checking vulnerable people, exits, and safe limits first.
Mixing clean and unsafe items during Mutual aid skills directory because labels, dates, or responsible names were not written down.
alternatives: Run a smaller version of Mutual aid skills directory with one responsible person, one helper, and one visible paper note.
Use a safer fallback location or method for Mutual aid skills directory if the first choice becomes wet, crowded, contaminated, dark, unstable, or unsafe.
Pause Mutual aid skills directory and verify the written record with a second person before moving scarce supplies.
failure_signs: People cannot explain who is responsible for Mutual aid skills directory or when the next check happens.
Supplies connected to Mutual aid skills directory are unlabelled, damp, contaminated, damaged, missing, or being used for the wrong purpose.
The routine ignores fire risk, carbon monoxide risk, tool condition, batteries, and safe isolation and starts creating new risk instead of reducing existing risk during “Mutual aid skills directory”
when_not_to_use: Do not use Mutual aid skills directory as a reason to delay evacuation, emergency calls, medical care, fire response, or official local instructions.
Do not use Mutual aid skills directory when the location is structurally unsafe, chemically suspicious, smoky, flooded, violent, or otherwise immediately dangerous.
Do not improvise hazardous repairs, dosing, chemical handling, weapon use, or electrical/gas work under the label of Mutual aid skills directory.
short_term: For the first hours of Mutual aid skills directory, keep the action small, visible, and reversible: write the goal, assign one owner, protect vulnerable people, separate safe from unsafe supplies, and set a review time.
long_term: If Mutual aid skills directory remains necessary for days or weeks, rotate roles, preserve written lessons, replace fragile supplies, and train another person so the group does not depend on one expert.
if_method_fails: If Mutual aid skills directory fails, stop escalation, move to the safest fallback, record what failed, protect people first, and restart only with a simpler written plan.
environment_notes: Adapt Mutual aid skills directory to the real environment: check fire risk, carbon monoxide risk, tool condition, batteries, and safe isolation, local weather, building condition, water access, light, noise, animals, neighbours, and safe exits before repeating the routine.
